text: A lot of people seem convinced that the point of AI coding is to write low-quality code as fast as possible.

evidence.quote: A lot of people seem convinced that the point of AI coding is to write low-quality code as fast as possible.

text: LLMs are flexible enough to be used to write high-quality code more slowly, not just to generate slop quickly.

evidence.quote: And you can use them just as effectively to write high-quality code more slowly.

issues.detail: 1 argument(s) not found in the quote/span — advisory only

text: LLM agents are really good at finding bugs.

evidence.quote: LLM agents are really good at finding bugs.

text: The latest public models from Anthropic and OpenAI are good enough to find plenty of bugs in an unscrutinized codebase.

evidence.quote: the latest public models from Anthropic and OpenAI are good enough to find plenty of bugs in an unscrutinized codebase.

arguments.surface: latest public models from Anthropic and OpenAI

text: The challenge with AI bug-finding is prioritizing and validating the findings, not just locating the bugs.

evidence.quote: The problem is not so much finding the bugs, but instead prioritizing and validating them.

issues.detail: 1 argument(s) not found in the quote/span — advisory only

text: Using multiple different models for PR review reduces the likelihood of hallucinations or bogus bugs.

evidence.quote: the more, different models you throw at a PR review, the less likely you are to get hallucinations or bogus bugs.

issues.detail: 1 argument(s) not found in the quote/span — advisory only

text: Run multiple AI agents (Claude sub-agent, Codex, and Cursor Bugbot) to find bugs ranked by critical/high/medium/low, then review findings, rule out false positives, and write a final report.

evidence.quote: Run a Claude sub-agent, Codex, and Cursor Bugbot to find bugs in this PR ranked by critical/high/medium/low. Once they're all done, review their findings, do your own research to rule out false positives, and write a final report.

arguments.surface: Claude sub-agent, Codex, and Cursor Bugbot

text: The multi-model bug-finding approach consistently finds many bugs with a near-zero false positive rate.

evidence.quote: this skill always finds tons of bugs in a PR, and the false positive rate is near zero.

text: Using the multi-model review technique doesn't necessarily increase velocity; it often reveals pre-existing bugs, leading to tangential fixes.

evidence.quote: When I use this technique, I haven't necessarily seen my velocity go up. If anything, the review process often finds pre-existing bugs, so I end up on a tangential side-quest where I'm writing unit tests and fixing subtle flaws that pre-date the PR.

text: The failure modes of a codebase are more informative to explore than its happy-path architecture.

evidence.quote: the happy-path of a complex architecture is less interesting than its failure modes.

text: Use a slower style of vibe coding by asking an agent how a PR works and how it might fail, using documentation with Mermaid charts and Matt Pocock's /grill-me skill to understand the PR fully.

evidence.quote: ask an agent how your PR works and how it might fail. Have it write Markdown docs with Mermaid charts if necessary. Use Matt Pocock's /grill-me skill until you understand the entire PR front-to-back.
